title: The Liberty Toast
description: Raise a glass (lemonade counts!) and give a one-sentence toast to freedom in front of at least two other guests.

history.event: First public readings of the Declaration
history.summary: After the Declaration of Independence was adopted, it was read aloud in town squares to cheering crowds, often accompanied by toasts, bonfires, and the ringing of bells.

title: The Boldest Signature
description: Trivia: Who signed the Declaration of Independence first, in a signature so large the British 'could read it without spectacles'? (Enter the full name.)

history.summary: John Hancock, President of the Continental Congress, signed first and largest. His name became American slang for a signature.

title: Paul Revere's Ride
description: Complete one lap of the backyard while shouting a warning of your choosing. Speed optional, drama required.

history.event: The Midnight Ride of Paul Revere
history.summary: Revere rode through the night to warn colonial militia that British forces were advancing, helping spark the battles of Lexington and Concord.

title: The Boston Tea Party
description: Toss a (provided) bean bag into the floating 'harbor' bucket from behind the line. A fellow guest must witness and verify your throw.

history.summary: Colonists disguised as Mohawk people dumped 342 chests of British tea into Boston Harbor to protest taxation without representation.

title: Star-Spangled Karaoke
description: Sing at least one line of a patriotic song loudly enough for the grill to hear. Have a witness verify your performance.

history.event: Francis Scott Key writes the anthem
history.summary: During the War of 1812, Key watched Fort McHenry survive a British bombardment and wrote the poem that became 'The Star-Spangled Banner.'

title: Betsy Ross Challenge
description: Assemble the mini flag puzzle at the craft table in under two minutes.

history.summary: Congress resolved that the flag have thirteen stripes and thirteen stars. Legend credits Betsy Ross with sewing an early version.

title: Let Freedom Ring
description: Trivia: In which U.S. city can you visit the Liberty Bell today? (Enter the city name.)

history.summary: Cast for the Pennsylvania State House (Independence Hall) in Philadelphia, the bell became an enduring symbol of American liberty and abolition.

title: The Freedom Feast
description: Try one item from each color of the red-white-and-blue snack spread.

history.summary: The first organized Fourth of July celebration in Philadelphia in 1777 featured a grand dinner, music, and fireworks — setting the template for the cookouts we hold today.

title: The Year It All Began
description: Trivia: In what year was the Declaration of Independence adopted? (Enter the four-digit year.)

history.summary: The Second Continental Congress adopted the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776 — the date we celebrate as Independence Day.

title: Fireworks Finale
description: Gather with the group for the fireworks and snap a team photo. A fellow guest verifies you made it to the grand finish of the Trail!

history.summary: Philadelphia marked the first anniversary of independence with fireworks. John Adams had predicted the day should be celebrated with 'illuminations from one end of this continent to the other.'
